By damaging America's main naval forces Japan was able
to take the advantage in Southeast Asia. Japan was
then able to proceed with its operations without the
threat of the Allied Pacific Fleet.
By demolishing the Allied forces stationed in Southeast
Asia, Japan gained access to the vast strategic
resources needed to solidify its front lines and
The success of this operation provided Japan the time to
fortify their air defenses and schedule the occupation
operation of Hawaii, home port of the United States
By successfully defeating enemy forces, Japan was able
to complicate future operations for the Allies who had
planned to use Australia as a base and provided Japan
with the upper hand in the Pacific.
Having won a decisive victory in the Mid-Pacific, Japan
had managed to create enough time to strengthen their
"Absolute Defense Belt" before American forces could
By achieving their primary goals in the SHO-1 opera-
tion, Japan proved that it had not yet been beaten
completely, but it was also clear that the Americans
would force much tougher battles upon them.
We used to be able to turn the Allied forces away
easily, but we are too weak now. The only choices we
have available to us now are continued resistance or a
Through its defense of Southeast Asia regions from
Japanese forces, Allied forces were able to drive Japan,
which lacked the necessary resources to maintain its
front line, into an even more difficult position.
The Allied forces, having successfully stopped the
invasion of the Japanese Army, were able to use their
success at Midway as a start for future counterattacks
Having won the battle at Midway, Allied forces were able
to foil the Japanese plan to split American and
Australian forces. This caused a shift in power, and
allowed Allied forces to take an offensive position.
The U.S. secured its dominance in the Pacific theater
with this victory and completely destroyed the "Absolute
Defense Belt" established by the Japanese. Now, the
Japanese forces are forced to take a defensive position.
By eliminating Japanese army and naval resistance,
American forces were able to enjoy victory. They
succeeded in isolating the Japanese mainland from its
southern territories, and narrowed its net around Japan.
Our victory against the remaining Japanese forces has
made it clear that there is nothing that can stop the
Allied forces. The only strategic operation remaining

On December 1, 1941, the leaders of Japan decided to
open war upon the U.S. on December 8. The negotiations
between U.S. Secretary of State, Cordell Hull, and the
Japanese Ambassadors, Kurusu and Nomura, had reached a
deadlock. An air fleet under the command of Vice Admiral
Nagumo had already departed from the Tankan Bay in the
Kuriles and was headed for Hawaii. The next day, Nagumo
received word from the Imperial Headquarters to proceed
with the attack on Pearl Harbor.
The attack had been devised by Isoroku Yamamoto,
Commander-in-Chief of the Combined Fleet. He believed
that the only way to defeat the U.S. was to launch a
surprise attack and deliver a swift and devastating blow
In 1932, Japan seized Manchuria and established a new
puppet regime called Manchukoku. Following their blue-
print for the "Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere",
Japan sought complete control over Eastern Asia; they
waged the "China Incident" and advanced into French
The expansionist policies adopted by Japan brought
them into direct conflict with the U.S., who immediately
responded with both economic and military action by
creating an oil embargo and moving command of the
Negotiations for peaceful settlement were opened, but
by November 1941, they had deadlocked, and the U.S. had
delivered an ultimatum with its "Hull Notes". Japan was
prepared to make a final and fatal decision.
After a successful surprise attack against Pearl
Harbor, the Japanese went on to score straight victories
against the ill-prepared Allied forces. They occupied
the Philippines, Malaya and then the Dutch East Indies
within a very short period of time. These early victo-
ries gave Japan such confidence that the Japanese felt
there was no reason to fear the Allies.
Furious over the assault on U.S. soil, American citi-
zens rallied under the motto "Remember Pearl Harbor",
and the armed forces waited for the chance to strike
As the U.S. recovered from their early mishaps in the
war, they built up their war machine and prepared for
On 7/20/43, the U.S. launched a two-front offensive
against Japan. Admiral Nimitz's Central Pacific Command
headed naval operations in the Mid Pacific and General
MacArthur's Southwest Command led army operations to
occupy territories from New Guinea to the Philippines.
Japan lost New Guinea and Attu Island after sustain-
ing heavy damages at their base in Rabaul. Faced with
few alternatives, the Japanese abandoned their expan-
sionist stance and adopted a defensive position. How-
ever, the Japanese lost several more bases within half a
year. Japan's defensive plans were crumbling.
The Japanese tried to regroup by launching Operation
A-GO and Operation Imphal. Military numbers favored the
Allies, but would the U.S. underestimate the spiritual
The attack on Pearl Harbor plunged Japan into war
with the United States. In search of precious supplies
and natural resources, Japanese forces launched their
Southeast Asia Offensive starting with the Philippines.
After delivering a severe blow to the U.S. Pacific
Fleet in Hawaii, commanders of the Japanese Fleet felt
that the success of their next operation would assure
Japan's survival in a long-term war.
Meanwhile, the Allied Army under the command of
General MacArthur, took a defensive position in the
Philippines. In Singapore, defenses were strengthened by
deploying the British Royal Navy's own Force Z, which
included the Prince of Wales, to Southeast Asia. The
first wave of crucial sea battles between the U.S. and
On April 18, 1942, sixteen B-25s took off from the USS
Hornet to bomb the capital city of Japan, Tokyo. The
physical damage caused by the Doolittle Raid was mini-
mal, but it caused great damage to Japan's public
psyche, and the message it sent to Japanese leaders was
profound. Sensing an immediate need to gain control of
the mid-Pacific, Admiral Yamamoto drew up a plan to
extend the Japanese perimeter and destroy U.S. task
forces with the occupation of Midway.
However, cryptanalysts in the U.S. had just recently
broken the Japanese Purple Code; giving Admiral Nimitz
an opportunity to deploy all available forces to Midway
and prepare for the attack. One month after the Battle
of the Coral Sea, Japanese and U.S. naval forces would
By mid-1942, the Japanese Imperial Navy had lost four
aircraft carriers at Midway and failed to occupy Port
Moresby. As part of an operation to sever communications
between the U.S. and Australia, the Japanese began
Recognizing the strategic importance of Australia, the
U.S. landed marines on the island on August 7, and took
the airfield before it could be completed.
Proudly re-named Henderson Field, it served as a
center of vicious fighting between U.S. and Japanese
forces for the next several months.
Both nations lost several vessels in the Solomons,
thus earning it the nickname Iron-Bottom Sound from the
U.S. forces had seized the initiative in the war and
forced Japan into a defensive position. Within less than
half a year, the U.S. had encroached on the outer shell
of Japan's defensive perimeter by significantly reducing
Japanese strength at Rabaul and occupying New Guinea and
On May 3, 1944, Japan's Imperial Headquarters began
Operation A-GO. The Japanese planned a massive air and
naval counterstrike by using long-range aircraft to
their advantage and challenging Admiral Nimitz's Central
Pacific Command from a distance outside that of U.S.
The operation's task force was made up of all remain-
ing Japanese carriers and twice as many aircraft as used
in the attack on Pearl Harbor.
On October 17, 1944, General MacArthur began the U.S.
landings at Leyte, with the support of naval task forces
under Admiral Halsey and Vice Admiral Kinkaid.
In May of 1942, when the occupation of Corregidor
Island forced General MacArthur to retreat, he promised
the people of the Philippines, "I shall return".
Those three words became the core of Gen. MacArthur's
strategy, and 2 years later he fulfilled his promise.
The Imperial Headquarters had predicted that the next
Allied objective would be the Philippines, and began
preparing for an invasion by launching Operation SHO-1.
The plan challenged the U.S. to a massive naval battle
involving high stakes for both sides.
With the onslaught of U.S. B-29s in Japan, the Imper-
ial forces were stretched to the limit. The Japanese GNP
had fallen dramatically and her citizens had been forced
to endure a life governed by tight rations.
The U.S. occupation of the Philippines had cut off
Japan's access to resources in the south and left the
Imperial Army and Navy short of everything from artill-
ery to fuel. Nevertheless, Japanese militarists showed
signs of fierce and reckless resistance. Willing to
sacrifice everything, about 2 million soldiers remained
in China, and the battleship Yamato stood in good condi-
tion. U.S. forces steadily moved in on the Japanese
mainland, first by launching invasions into Iwo Jima and
Naha. Japan's defeat was inevitable, but the final days
of fighting would be costly for all.
